preamble.summary: Samuel delivers the LORD's command to Saul: march against Amalek and execute total cherem — devoted destruction of everything and everyone. Saul wages a successful campaign, crushing the Amalekite forces from Havilah to Shur. But he spares King Agag alive and allows his troops to keep the best livestock, claiming the animals are for sacrifice to the LORD. God tells Samuel he regrets making Saul king. Samuel confronts Saul at Gilgal with one of the most devastating theological verdicts in the Hebrew Bible: 'To obey is better than sacrifice, and to listen than the fat of rams.' Saul is rejected as king. He begs Samuel not to humiliate him publicly, and Samuel briefly accompanies him — but as Samuel turns to leave, Saul tears his robe, and Samuel declares that the LORD has torn the kingdom from him and given it to a neighbor who is better. Samuel then personally executes Agag before the LORD at Gilgal. The two men part and never see each other again, though Samuel grieves over Saul for the rest of his life.
preamble.remarkable: This chapter contains what may be the single most important theological statement about worship in the entire Hebrew Bible: hashmoa' mizzevach tov lehaqshiv mechelev eilim — 'to obey is better than sacrifice, to listen than the fat of rams' (v22). This is not a late prophetic innovation; it is placed in the mouth of the last judge and first prophet of the monarchical period, at the very inception of kingship. The statement does not abolish sacrifice — it subordinates it. Ritual without obedience is not merely insufficient; it is rebellion (meri) and divination (qesem). Samuel equates Saul's self-willed 'worship' with the pagan practices Israel was commanded to destroy. The chapter also introduces an extraordinary theological tension around the verb nacham ('relent, repent, be grieved'). In verse 11, the LORD says 'I regret (nichamti) that I made Saul king.' In verse 29, Samuel declares 'the Eternal One of Israel does not lie and does not relent (yinnachem), for he is not a human being that he should relent (lehinnachem).' Then in verse 35, the narrator states 'the LORD regretted (nicham) that he had made Saul king.' The same verb is both affirmed and denied of God within a single chapter. This is not carelessness — it is the text wrestling with divine sovereignty and divine grief simultaneously, refusing to resolve the tension.
preamble.friction: The cherem command against Amalek raises profound moral questions that the text itself does not soften. The instruction in verse 3 is comprehensive: kill men, women, children, infants, and all livestock. Modern readers recoil; ancient readers would have understood cherem as the total consecration of war spoil to God — nothing may be profited from, everything belongs to the divine realm. The tension is compounded by the fact that Saul is condemned not for excessive violence but for insufficient obedience to the destruction order. Critical scholarship identifies multiple layers: the Amalekite conflict tradition (Exodus 17:8-16, Deuteronomy 25:17-19), the Deuteronomistic theological framework that interprets Saul's reign as failed, and the possible retrojection of later anti-Amalekite sentiment. The Kenites receiving advance warning (v6) shows that the narrator recognizes ethical distinctions within the campaign — innocents can be spared. The nacham tension (vv11, 29, 35) has generated centuries of theological debate: does God change his mind? The text presents both positions without harmonizing them, suggesting that divine constancy and divine responsiveness to human action are both true and irreducible.
preamble.connections: The Amalekite war connects directly to Exodus 17:8-16, where Amalek attacked Israel's rear — the weak, the exhausted, the stragglers — during the wilderness journey, and to Deuteronomy 25:17-19, which commands Israel to 'blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven.' Saul's failure to complete cherem mirrors Achan's violation of cherem at Jericho (Joshua 7), where one man's retention of devoted goods brought disaster on all Israel. The verb ma'as ('reject') creates a devastating wordplay: because Saul rejected (ma'asta) the word of the LORD, the LORD has rejected (vayyima'asekha) him as king — the punishment mirrors the crime in the same root. Samuel's statement about obedience and sacrifice anticipates the prophetic tradition of Hosea 6:6 ('I desire loyal love and not sacrifice'), Isaiah 1:11-17, Amos 5:21-24, Micah 6:6-8, and Psalm 51:16-17. The tearing of the robe in verse 27 becomes a recurring symbol of torn kingship: Ahijah will tear a garment into twelve pieces before Jeroboam (1 Kings 11:30-31). Agag's death at Gilgal brings the Amalekite thread to a temporary close, but Amalek resurfaces: David will fight them (1 Samuel 30), and an Amalekite will claim to have killed Saul (2 Samuel 1:8-10). The Amalekite line persists all the way to Haman the Agagite in Esther — a descendant of the royal house Saul was supposed to destroy.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵל֙ אֶל־שָׁא֔וּל אֹתִ֨י שָׁלַ֤ח יְהֹוָה֙ לִמְשׇׁחֳךָ֣ לְמֶ֔לֶךְ עַל־עַמּ֖וֹ עַל־יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל וְעַתָּ֣ה שְׁמַ֔ע לְק֖וֹל דִּבְרֵ֥י יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Samuel also said unto Saul, The LORD sent me to anoint thee to be king over his people, over Israel: now therefore hearken unto the voice of the words of the LORD.
verses.rendering: Samuel said to Saul, "The LORD sent me to anoint you as king over his people, over Israel. Now listen to the voice of the LORD's words.
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel opens by grounding his authority — and Saul's obligation — in the original anointing. The logic is devastating in its simplicity: I am the one who made you king, on the LORD's authority; therefore you owe your throne to obedience. The phrase shema' leqol divrei YHWH ('listen to the voice of the LORD's words') uses shema', the great verb of Israelite covenant faithfulness. This is not a suggestion; it is a covenant demand. The entire chapter will hinge on whether Saul does or does not shema'. Samuel is not introducing new obligations — he is recalling the foundational one.
translator_notes: The opening oti shalach YHWH ('me the LORD sent') places the pronoun oti ('me') in emphatic first position — Samuel is asserting his prophetic credentials before delivering a divine command. The verb limshochakha ('to anoint you') recalls the private anointing of 1 Samuel 10:1. The phrase al-ammo al-Yisra'el ('over his people, over Israel') uses double specification: the people belong to God first, and Israel is their name. The imperative shema' ('hear, listen, obey') is the same root that will appear in verse 22 as the chapter's theological climax. Samuel front-loads the key verb: everything depends on hearing.

key_terms.semantic_range: hear, listen, obey, heed, pay attention, understand
key_terms.note: Shama' is the foundational verb of Israelite covenant life — the opening word of the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4). In Hebrew, hearing and obeying are not separate concepts; genuine hearing produces obedience. This chapter will indict Saul precisely on the grounds that he heard the LORD's command but did not shama' — did not obey. The verb appears here as imperative, in verse 22 as theological principle, and throughout the chapter as the measure of faithfulness.

verses.text_hebrew: כֹּ֤ה אָמַר֙ יְהֹוָ֣ה צְבָא֔וֹת פָּקַ֕דְתִּי אֵ֛ת אֲשֶׁר־עָשָׂ֥ה עֲמָלֵ֖ק לְיִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל אֲשֶׁר־שָׂ֥ם ל֛וֹ בַּדֶּ֖רֶךְ בַּעֲלֹת֥וֹ מִמִּצְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thus saith the LORD of hosts, I remember that which Amalek did to Israel, how he laid wait for him in the way, when he came up from Egypt.
verses.rendering: This is what the LORD of Armies says: 'I have called to account what Amalek did to Israel — how he set an ambush against him on the road when he came up from Egypt.
verses.expanded_rendering: The divine title YHWH tseva'ot ('the LORD of Armies') appears here for a military commission — the God who commands heaven's armies is now commissioning Israel's army. The verb paqadti ('I have called to account, I have attended to') does not mean 'I remember' in a passive sense; it means 'I have taken official notice and will now act.' God frames the Amalekite war as delayed justice for a specific crime: the ambush at Rephidim (Exodus 17:8-16), where Amalek attacked Israel's stragglers — the weak, the exhausted, those who could not keep up. Deuteronomy 25:18 adds that Amalek 'did not fear God.' This is not arbitrary aggression but the execution of a sentence pronounced centuries earlier.
translator_notes: The messenger formula koh amar YHWH ('thus says the LORD') marks this as direct prophetic oracle — Samuel speaks as God's mouthpiece. The title tseva'ot ('of armies/hosts') encompasses both heavenly and earthly forces; its use here militarizes the context. The verb paqadti from p-q-d ('to visit, attend to, call to account, muster') carries judicial force — God has reviewed the case and reached a verdict. The phrase asher-sam lo baderekh ('who set [an ambush] for him on the road') refers to Amalek's attack during the Exodus journey. The participle implies premeditated aggression: Amalek chose to attack Israel on the vulnerable march from Egypt.

verses.text_hebrew: עַתָּה֩ לֵ֨ךְ וְהִכִּֽיתָ֜ה אֶת־עֲמָלֵ֗ק וְהַֽחֲרַמְתֶּם֙ אֶת־כׇּל־אֲשֶׁר־ל֔וֹ וְלֹ֥א תַחְמֹ֖ל עָלָ֑יו וְהֵמַתָּ֞ה מֵאִ֣ישׁ עַד־אִשָּׁ֗ה מֵעֹלֵל֙ וְעַד־יוֹנֵ֔ק מִשּׁ֣וֹר וְעַד־שֶׂ֔ה מִגָּמָ֖ל וְעַד־חֲמֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: Now go and smite Amalek, and utterly destroy all that they have, and spare them not; but slay both man and woman, infant and suckling, ox and sheep, camel and ass.
verses.rendering: Now go and strike Amalek. Devote to destruction everything that belongs to him. Do not show him pity. Kill man and woman, child and nursing infant, ox and sheep, camel and donkey.'"
verses.expanded_rendering: The command is cherem — total devoted destruction, the most extreme category of warfare in Israelite law. Everything and everyone belonging to Amalek is to be consecrated to God through destruction; nothing may be kept, nothing may be profited from. The comprehensive list — man and woman, child and nursing infant, ox and sheep, camel and donkey — eliminates any ambiguity about the scope. The verb lo tachmo ('do not show pity') explicitly forbids the kind of mercy Saul will exercise toward Agag and the livestock. Cherem treats the entire enemy population and its possessions as belonging to the divine realm, removed from human use. In the Israelite theological framework, to retain cherem goods is not generosity — it is theft from God.
translator_notes: The verb vehachramtem from ch-r-m ('devote to destruction, place under the ban') is the Hiphil imperative plural — the entire army is commanded to execute cherem. This is the same root used for the destruction of Jericho (Joshua 6:17-21). The phrase lo tachmo alav ('do not show him pity') uses ch-m-l ('to spare, have compassion'), preemptively forbidding exactly what Saul will do. The merism me'ish ad-ishah me'olel ve'ad-yoneq ('from man to woman, from child to nursing infant') covers the entire human population. The livestock list (mishor ve'ad-seh migamal ve'ad-chamor) covers the entire economic wealth of a pastoral-nomadic society.

key_terms.semantic_range: ban, devoted thing, total destruction, consecration to God through annihilation
key_terms.note: Cherem is not ordinary warfare — it is sacral destruction, the complete offering of war spoil to God by eliminating it from human possession. The root ch-r-m means to set apart, to consecrate by removing from common use. In practice, this meant killing all people and animals and burning all goods. Retaining any cherem item was not pragmatism but sacrilege — a violation of God's exclusive claim on the devoted material. Achan's violation of cherem at Jericho (Joshua 7) provides the interpretive backdrop: one man's retention of banned goods brought divine judgment on all Israel.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְשַׁמַּ֤ע שָׁאוּל֙ אֶת־הָעָ֔ם וַיִּפְקְדֵ֖ם בַּטְּלָאִ֑ים מָאתַ֤יִם אֶ֙לֶף֙ רַגְלִ֔י וַעֲשֶׂ֥רֶת אֲלָפִ֖ים אֶת־אִ֥ישׁ יְהוּדָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Saul gathered the people together, and numbered them in Telaim, two hundred thousand footmen, and ten thousand men of Judah.
verses.rendering: Saul summoned the people and mustered them at Telaim: two hundred thousand foot soldiers, along with ten thousand men of Judah.
translator_notes: The verb vayeshamma' ('he summoned, he caused to hear') is the Piel of sh-m-' — Saul 'makes the people hear' the call to war. Telaim is likely in the southern Negev, near Amalekite territory. The separate count of Judah from the rest of Israel (as in 11:8) again reflects the political distinction between the southern and northern tribes. The numbers (200,000 foot soldiers plus 10,000 from Judah) are large; as with other biblical military censuses, elef may indicate 'military unit/clan' rather than the literal number 1,000. The verb vayyifqedem ('he mustered them') from p-q-d echoes verse 2, where God uses the same root to 'call Amalek to account.'

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּבֹ֥א שָׁא֖וּל עַד־עִ֣יר עֲמָלֵ֑ק וַיָּ֖רֶב בַּנָּֽחַל׃
verses.text_kjv: And Saul came to a city of Amalek, and laid wait in the valley.
verses.rendering: Saul advanced to the city of Amalek and set an ambush in the wadi.
translator_notes: The phrase ir Amaleq ('city of Amalek') likely refers to the principal settlement or capital of the Amalekite confederation. The verb vayyarev ('he set an ambush') from '-r-v shows tactical planning — Saul conceals forces in the nachal ('wadi, dry riverbed, seasonal streambed'), which is characteristic of Negev terrain and provides natural cover for concealed troops. The irony is sharp: Amalek ambushed Israel on the road from Egypt (v2), and now Saul ambushes Amalek. The same verb root ('-r-v) connects the two actions.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר שָׁא֣וּל אֶֽל־הַקֵּינִ֡י לְכוּ֩ סֻּ֨רוּ רְד֜וּ מִתּ֣וֹךְ עֲמָלֵקִ֗י פֶּן־אֹֽסִפְךָ֙ עִמּ֔וֹ וְאַתָּ֞ה עָשִׂ֤יתָה חֶ֙סֶד֙ עִם־כׇּל־בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל בַּעֲלוֹתָ֖ם מִמִּצְרָ֑יִם וַיָּ֥סַר קֵינִ֖י מִתּ֥וֹךְ עֲמָלֵֽק׃
verses.text_kjv: And Saul said unto the Kenites, Go, depart, get you down from among the Amalekites, lest I destroy you with them: for ye shewed kindness to all the children of Israel, when they came up out of Egypt. So the Kenites departed from among the Amalekites.
verses.rendering: Saul said to the Kenites, "Go — withdraw — get away from the Amalekites before I sweep you away with them, because you showed loyal kindness to all the Israelites when they came up from Egypt." So the Kenites withdrew from among the Amalekites.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is critically important for understanding the ethics of cherem as the text presents them. Saul distinguishes between guilty Amalekites and innocent Kenites living among them — he takes care to evacuate non-combatants who have a history of covenant loyalty to Israel. The Kenites are descendants of Hobab/Jethro, Moses' father-in-law (Judges 1:16, 4:11), who showed chesed ('loyal kindness, covenant faithfulness') to Israel during the Exodus. Saul recognizes that cherem against Amalek does not require the destruction of everyone in the geographical area — only those under the ban. The narrator implicitly approves this ethical distinction. What makes Saul's later failure so damning is the contrast: he shows proper discernment here by sparing the innocent, but then shows improper mercy by sparing the guilty king and the best livestock.
translator_notes: The triple imperative lekhu suru redu ('go, turn aside, go down') conveys urgency — Saul wants the Kenites out before the attack begins. The verb pen-osifkha ('lest I gather you up / sweep you away') from '-s-p warns of collateral destruction. The word chesed ('loyal kindness, covenant faithfulness') is one of the richest terms in Hebrew — it denotes not mere kindness but obligation-based loyalty within a relationship. The Kenites' chesed toward Israel during the Exodus creates an enduring claim on Israel's protection. The verb vayyasar ('he withdrew') shows the Kenites comply — the separation of innocent from guilty is accomplished before the battle.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion, steadfast love
key_terms.note: Chesed is the defining virtue of covenant relationship in the Hebrew Bible — it combines loyalty, kindness, and obligation. The Kenites showed chesed to Israel during the Exodus, and that act of faithfulness now protects them centuries later. Chesed creates enduring bonds and enduring debts. Saul's recognition of Kenite chesed shows he understands relational obligation — which makes his failure to obey God's direct command all the more culpable.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּ֥ךְ שָׁא֖וּל אֶת־עֲמָלֵ֑ק מֵחֲוִילָ֛ה בּוֹאֲךָ֥ שׁ֖וּר אֲשֶׁ֥ר עַל־פְּנֵ֥י מִצְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Saul smote the Amalekites from Havilah until thou comest to Shur, that is over against Egypt.
verses.rendering: Saul struck the Amalekites from Havilah all the way to Shur, which is east of Egypt.
translator_notes: The geographical markers describe a vast sweep of territory. Havilah is in the Arabian interior (possibly eastern Arabia or northern Yemen); Shur is the wilderness region east of Egypt's border (the Sinai). The phrase bo'akha Shur ('as you approach Shur') uses the standard directional idiom. The phrase asher al-penei Mitsrayim ('which is on the face of / opposite Egypt') locates Shur on Egypt's eastern frontier. This merism — from Havilah to Shur — indicates that Saul's campaign covered the entire range of Amalekite territory. The military victory is comprehensive; Saul's failure lies not in the battle but in its aftermath.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּתְפֹּ֛שׂ אֶת־אֲגַ֥ג מֶֽלֶךְ־עֲמָלֵ֖ק חָ֑י וְאֶת־כׇּל־הָעָ֖ם הֶחֱרִ֥ים לְפִי־חָֽרֶב׃
verses.text_kjv: And he took Agag the king of the Amalekites alive, and utterly destroyed all the people with the edge of the sword.
verses.rendering: He captured Agag king of Amalek alive, but he devoted all the people to destruction by the edge of the sword.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the pivot point of the entire chapter, stated with devastating brevity. Two clauses, sharply contrasted: Agag captured alive (chai) — the people devoted to destruction (hecherim lefi-charev). Saul executes cherem on the common people but exempts the king. The text does not yet explain Saul's motivation — that will emerge in his dialogue with Samuel — but the narrator's juxtaposition makes the violation unmistakable. The word chai ('alive') stands in direct contradiction to the command of verse 3. Saul has decided that cherem applies selectively, that a king is worth preserving. Whether this is political calculation (a captured king enhances Saul's prestige), misguided mercy, or sheer self-will, the result is the same: partial obedience, which the chapter will declare to be no obedience at all.
translator_notes: The verb vayyitpos ('he captured, he seized') implies taking prisoner rather than killing. The adjective chai ('alive') is emphatic — the text stresses that Agag was not killed. The title melekh Amaleq ('king of Amalek') identifies Agag as the royal figure whose survival most directly violates cherem. The phrase hecherim lefi-charev ('devoted to destruction by the mouth/edge of the sword') combines the cherem root (ch-r-m) with the standard expression for death by sword. The contrast between chai for the king and hecherim for the people is the structural hinge of the narrative.

key_terms.semantic_range: banned, placed under cherem, totally destroyed, consecrated through annihilation
key_terms.note: The Hiphil of ch-r-m — the active execution of the cherem ban. Saul applies it to the people but not to the king, creating a fatal inconsistency. The verb appears here in direct tension with chai ('alive'): the people are hecherim, Agag is chai. This selective application of God's command is the core of Saul's failure.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּחְמֹ֣ל שָׁא֣וּל וְהָעָ֡ם עַל־אֲגָג֩ וְעַל־מֵיטַ֨ב הַצֹּ֜אן וְהַבָּקָ֗ר וְהַמִּשְׁנִים֙ וְעַל־הַכָּרִ֔ים וְעַל־כׇּל־הַטּ֖וֹב וְלֹ֣א אָב֣וּ הַחֲרִימָ֑ם וְכׇל־הַמְּלָאכָ֛ה נְמִבְזָ֥ה וְנָמֵ֖ס אֹתָ֥הּ הֶחֱרִֽימוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: But Saul and the people spared Agag, and the best of the sheep, and of the oxen, and of the fatlings, and the lambs, and all that was good, and would not utterly destroy them: but every thing that was vile and refuse, that they destroyed utterly.
verses.rendering: But Saul and the people showed pity on Agag and on the best of the flock and the herd — the fattened animals, the lambs, and everything of value. They were unwilling to devote these to destruction. Only what was worthless and unwanted did they devote to destruction.
verses.expanded_rendering: The narrator exposes the self-serving logic of Saul's selective obedience. The verb vayachmo ('he showed pity') is the exact verb God prohibited in verse 3 (lo tachmo — 'do not show pity'). Saul does the one thing he was explicitly told not to do. But notice the pattern: he spares Agag (a king — politically valuable) and the best livestock (meitav — 'the choicest, the finest') and everything described as tov ('good, valuable'). What he destroys is only what is nimvezah venameis ('despised and worthless'). This is not mercy — it is economic calculation dressed in religious language. Saul 'devotes to destruction' only what he would have discarded anyway. The narrator's verdict is implicit in the description: Saul kept what he wanted and destroyed what he didn't. That is not obedience to God; it is obedience to appetite.
translator_notes: The verb vayachmo ('he showed pity, he spared') from ch-m-l directly violates the prohibition lo tachmo of verse 3. The word meitav ('the best, the choicest') from y-t-v emphasizes that Saul retained only premium goods. The word mishneim is debated — possibly 'second-born' animals (fattened for quality) or 'well-fed' livestock. The karim ('fattened lambs') are choice sacrificial-quality animals. The phrase lo avu hacharimam ('they were unwilling to devote them to destruction') reveals volition — this is not accidental oversight but deliberate refusal. The contrast terms nimvezah ('despised, contemptible') and nameis ('melting away, wasting, worthless') describe the rejected dregs. The chapter's moral calculus is razor-sharp: Saul destroyed trash and called it obedience.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֥י דְבַר־יְהֹוָ֖ה אֶל־שְׁמוּאֵ֥ל לֵאמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Then came the word of the LORD unto Samuel, saying,
verses.rendering: Then the word of the LORD came to Samuel:
translator_notes: The prophetic reception formula vayehi devar-YHWH el-Shemu'el ('the word of the LORD came to Samuel') marks a direct divine communication. This is the same formula used throughout the prophetic literature (Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Hosea) to introduce divine speech. Samuel will receive God's verdict on Saul's actions before confronting Saul himself. The verse is brief — a narrative hinge shifting the scene from the battlefield to the prophetic chamber.

verses.text_hebrew: נִחַ֗מְתִּי כִּֽי־הִמְלַ֤כְתִּי אֶת־שָׁאוּל֙ לְמֶ֔לֶךְ כִּֽי־שָׁ֤ב מֵאַֽחֲרַי֙ וְאֶת־דְּבָרַ֣י לֹ֣א הֵקִ֔ים וַיִּ֙חַר֙ לִשְׁמוּאֵ֔ל וַיִּזְעַ֥ק אֶל־יְהֹוָ֖ה כׇּל־הַלָּֽיְלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: It repenteth me that I have set up Saul to be king: for he is turned back from following me, and hath not performed my commandments. And it grieved Samuel, and he cried unto the LORD all night.
verses.rendering: "I regret that I made Saul king, because he has turned away from following me and has not carried out my commands." Samuel was deeply grieved, and he cried out to the LORD all night long.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse introduces one of the most theologically charged words in the chapter: nichamti ('I regret, I am grieved, I relent'). God declares regret over making Saul king. The verb nacham does not mean God made a mistake in the sense of miscalculation — it expresses the divine grief that arises when a human being fails to live up to the role God gave him. God's purposes are responsive to human choices; Saul's turning (shav me'acharai, 'turned back from following me') produces a corresponding divine response. Samuel's reaction is equally intense: vayyichar ('he burned' — the same word used for anger, here meaning deep distress) and vayyiz'aq el-YHWH kol-hallailah ('he cried out to the LORD all night'). Samuel does not accept God's verdict calmly; he agonizes over it, interceding through the entire night. This is a prophet who loves the king he is about to condemn.
translator_notes: The verb nichamti from n-ch-m is the theological crux of the chapter. It appears here (v11), is denied of God in verse 29, and reaffirmed in verse 35 — creating an unresolved tension the text does not harmonize. The root can mean 'regret, relent, change one's mind, be grieved, be consoled.' The phrase shav me'acharai ('he turned back from after me') uses the language of covenant defection — Saul has abandoned the path of following God. The phrase devarai lo heqim ('my words he has not carried out') uses q-w-m in the Hiphil ('to establish, fulfill, carry out'). The verb vayyichar ('it burned/grieved') for Samuel uses the same root as anger (ch-r-h) but here expresses anguished distress. The phrase vayyiz'aq el-YHWH kol-hallailah ('he cried out to the LORD all night') shows sustained, desperate intercession.

key_terms.semantic_range: repent, relent, regret, be grieved, change one's mind, be consoled
key_terms.note: Nacham applied to God creates a profound theological tension. In verse 11 and verse 35, God 'regrets' making Saul king. In verse 29, Samuel declares God 'does not relent.' The Hebrew text holds both statements without resolution, suggesting that divine constancy (God does not capriciously change) and divine responsiveness (God genuinely grieves human failure) are simultaneously true. The verb does not imply error but expresses the real emotional dimension of God's engagement with human history.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּשְׁכֵּ֧ם שְׁמוּאֵ֛ל לִקְרַ֥את שָׁא֖וּל בַּבֹּ֑קֶר וַיֻּגַּ֤ד לִשְׁמוּאֵל֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר בָּא שָׁא֣וּל הַכַּרְמֶ֗לָה וְהִנֵּ֤ה מַצִּיב֙ ל֣וֹ יָ֔ד וַיִּסֹּ֥ב וַיַּעֲבֹ֖ר וַיֵּ֥רֶד הַגִּלְגָּֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And when Samuel rose early to meet Saul in the morning, it was told Samuel, saying, Saul came to Carmel, and, behold, he set him up a place, and is gone about, and passed on, and gone down to Gilgal.
verses.rendering: Samuel rose early to meet Saul in the morning, but he was told, "Saul went to Carmel, and there he set up a monument for himself. Then he turned and moved on down to Gilgal."
verses.expanded_rendering: After a sleepless night of intercession, Samuel goes to confront Saul — only to learn that Saul has gone to Carmel (a town in the Judean hills, not Mount Carmel in the north) to erect a yad ('hand, monument, memorial') for himself. The word yad literally means 'hand' but here refers to a victory stele or commemorative pillar. Saul's first action after the battle is not worship, not thanksgiving, not consultation with the prophet — it is self-glorification. He builds a monument to his own victory. This detail, almost offhand in the narrative, reveals the interior disposition that made Saul's disobedience inevitable: he sees the war as his accomplishment, not God's command faithfully executed. He then moves to Gilgal — the site of his kingship renewal (11:14-15) and the place where Samuel will now tear that kingship away.
translator_notes: The verb vayyashkem ('he rose early') shows Samuel's urgency — he has been awake all night and moves at first light. The phrase matsiv lo yad ('setting up a hand/monument for himself') is telling: yad as 'monument' appears in 2 Samuel 18:18 for Absalom's memorial pillar. The reflexive lo ('for himself') is the narrator's implicit critique — Saul commemorates himself. Carmel (hakkarmelah) is a town in the Judean highlands (modern Khirbet el-Kirmil), not the northern Mount Carmel. Gilgal is the site of Saul's kingship ceremony (11:15) and will now become the site of his kingship's undoing — a savage geographical irony.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּבֹ֥א שְׁמוּאֵ֖ל אֶל־שָׁא֑וּל וַיֹּ֤אמֶר ל֨וֹ שָׁא֜וּל בָּר֤וּךְ אַתָּה֙ לַֽיהֹוָ֔ה הֲקִימֹ֖תִי אֶת־דְּבַ֥ר יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel came to Saul: and Saul said unto him, Blessed be thou of the LORD: I have performed the commandment of the LORD.
verses.rendering: Samuel came to Saul, and Saul said to him, "Blessed are you by the LORD! I have carried out the word of the LORD."
verses.expanded_rendering: Saul greets Samuel with a blessing formula and a declaration of completed obedience — haqimoti et-devar YHWH ('I have carried out the word of the LORD'). This is either breathtaking self-deception or deliberate dishonesty. The verb haqimoti ('I have established, carried out, fulfilled') is the same root God used in verse 11 to say Saul lo heqim ('has not carried out') his commands. Saul claims fulfillment; God has already declared failure. The greeting barukh attah laYHWH ('blessed are you by the LORD') may be an attempt to preempt confrontation — Saul speaks first, controls the narrative, and presents himself as the obedient servant. But Samuel is not fooled. What follows is one of the most devastating prophetic cross-examinations in Scripture.
translator_notes: The greeting barukh attah laYHWH ('blessed are you by the LORD') is a standard blessing formula, but in context it functions as deflection — Saul leads with piety. The phrase haqimoti et-devar YHWH ('I have carried out the LORD's word') uses the Hiphil of q-w-m ('to cause to stand, to establish, to fulfill'). This is precisely the verb God denied of Saul in verse 11 (devarai lo heqim). The contradiction between God's assessment and Saul's self-assessment could not be sharper. Saul does not say 'most of' or 'largely' — he claims complete fulfillment.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵ֔ל וּמֶ֛ה קוֹל־הַצֹּ֥אן הַזֶּ֖ה בְּאׇזְנָ֑י וְק֣וֹל הַבָּקָ֔ר אֲשֶׁ֥ר אָנֹכִ֖י שֹׁמֵֽעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel said, What meaneth then this bleating of the sheep in mine ears, and the lowing of the oxen which I hear?
verses.rendering: Samuel said, "Then what is this sound of sheep in my ears? And the sound of cattle that I am hearing?"
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the most brilliantly understated lines in the Hebrew Bible. Samuel does not accuse, does not cite God's verdict, does not launch into a prophetic oracle. He simply asks about the noise. The sheep are bleating. The cattle are lowing. If Saul has carried out the LORD's word — if cherem was fully executed — there should be silence. The animals themselves testify against Saul. Samuel uses the verb shomea' ('hearing') — the same root (sh-m-') that means 'obey' — with savage irony: 'I am hearing sheep and cattle, but I am not hearing obedience.' The evidence of Saul's failure is audible, undeniable, and bleating in Samuel's ears.
translator_notes: The phrase qol-hatson hazzeh be'oznai ('this sound of sheep in my ears') makes the evidence sensory and immediate — Samuel is not citing a report but hearing living proof. The word qol ('voice, sound') is the same word used in verse 1 for the 'voice' of God's words (qol divrei YHWH). Samuel's ears should contain God's voice; instead they contain sheep sounds. The verb shomea' ('hearing') is a participle of sh-m-' — the chapter's governing verb. The question is rhetorical: Samuel knows exactly what the sounds mean. He is forcing Saul to account for the discrepancy between his claim and the audible evidence.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר שָׁאוּל֙ מֵעֲמָלֵקִ֣י הֱבִיא֔וּם אֲשֶׁ֨ר חָמַ֤ל הָעָם֙ עַל־מֵיטַ֤ב הַצֹּאן֙ וְהַבָּקָ֔ר לְמַ֥עַן זְבֹ֖חַ לַיהֹוָ֣ה אֱלֹהֶ֑יךָ וְאֶת־הַיּוֹתֵ֖ר הֶחֱרַֽמְנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And Saul said, They have brought them from the Amalekites: for the people spared the best of the sheep and of the oxen, to sacrifice unto the LORD thy God; and the rest we have utterly destroyed.
verses.rendering: Saul said, "They brought them from the Amalekites, because the people showed pity on the best of the sheep and cattle in order to sacrifice them to the LORD your God. But the rest we devoted to destruction."
verses.expanded_rendering: Saul's defense contains three revealing moves. First, he deflects blame: 'they brought them' — the people did this, not me. Second, he spiritualizes the violation: the livestock was spared lema'an zevoach laYHWH Elohekha ('in order to sacrifice to the LORD your God'). Disobedience is rebranded as worship. Third, he uses a pronoun shift that may be the most damning detail: 'the LORD your God' — not 'our God' or 'my God' but Elohekha, 'your God,' as though the LORD belongs to Samuel's sphere and not his own. Saul is distancing himself from God even as he claims to act in God's name. The rest, he assures Samuel, was properly destroyed — which the narrator has already told us (v9) means only the worthless and unwanted portion.
translator_notes: The subject shift from 'I have carried out' (v13, first person singular) to 'they brought them' (hevi'um, third person plural) is a transparent attempt to transfer responsibility. The verb chamal ('showed pity, spared') is the same prohibited verb from verse 3 and described in verse 9. The phrase lema'an zevoach laYHWH Elohekha ('in order to sacrifice to the LORD your God') wraps disobedience in liturgical language. The pronoun Elohekha ('your God') rather than Elohai ('my God') or Elohenu ('our God') creates theological distance — Saul treats God as Samuel's deity rather than his own sovereign. The phrase ve'et-hayyoter hecheramnu ('and the rest we devoted to destruction') quietly admits that cherem was applied only to what remained after the best was removed.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵל֙ אֶל־שָׁא֔וּל הֶ֕רֶף וְאַגִּ֣ידָה לְּךָ֔ אֵ֛ת אֲשֶׁ֥ר דִּבֶּ֛ר יְהֹוָ֥ה אֵלַ֖י הַלָּ֑יְלָה וַיֹּ֥אמֶר ל֖וֹ דַּבֵּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Then Samuel said unto Saul, Stay, and I will tell thee what the LORD hath said to me this night. And he said unto him, Say on.
verses.rendering: Samuel said to Saul, "Stop. Let me tell you what the LORD said to me last night." Saul said to him, "Speak."
translator_notes: The imperative heref ('stop, cease, let go') cuts Saul off — Samuel has heard enough of his excuses. The phrase et asher dibber YHWH elai hallailah ('what the LORD spoke to me last night') refers to the revelation of verse 11. Samuel has been carrying this word all night, agonizing over it, and now delivers it. Saul's response dabber ('speak') is a single word — perhaps confident ignorance, perhaps growing dread. The brevity of both speakers creates taut narrative tension.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵ֔ל הֲל֗וֹא אִם־קָטֹ֤ן אַתָּה֙ בְּעֵינֶ֔יךָ רֹ֛אשׁ שִׁבְטֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל אָ֑תָּה וַיִּמְשָׁחֲךָ֧ יְהֹוָ֛ה לְמֶ֖לֶךְ עַל־יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel said, When thou wast little in thine own sight, wast thou not made the head of the tribes of Israel, and the LORD anointed thee king over Israel?
verses.rendering: Samuel said, "Were you not small in your own eyes when you were made head of the tribes of Israel? The LORD anointed you king over Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel reaches back to the beginning of Saul's story — to the young man who protested that his tribe was the smallest in Israel and his clan the least significant (9:21). Once, Saul considered himself qaton ('small, insignificant'). That humility was the ground of his selection: God chose a man who did not seek greatness. Now that man has built himself a victory monument, blamed his troops for his own failure, and decided which parts of God's commands to obey. Samuel's question is not merely rhetorical — it is a lament for the man Saul used to be. The anointing (vayyimshachakha YHWH) was not Saul's achievement; it was God's sovereign act. Everything Saul has — his throne, his army, his authority — came from a God he has now disobeyed.
translator_notes: The phrase qaton attah be'einekha ('small in your own eyes') recalls 9:21 and 10:22 (Saul hiding among the baggage). The rhetorical question halo' ('is it not the case that...?') expects affirmation — Saul cannot deny his humble origins. The phrase rosh shivtei Yisra'el ('head of the tribes of Israel') identifies Saul's position as representative of the entire nation. The verb vayyimshachakha ('the LORD anointed you') repeats the claim of verse 1 — the anointing came from God, and God's authority over the anointed one is absolute.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּשְׁלָחֲךָ֣ יְהֹוָ֔ה בְּדָ֑רֶךְ וַיֹּ֗אמֶר לֵ֣ךְ וְהַחֲרַמְתָּ֞ה אֶת־הַחַטָּאִ֤ים אֶת־עֲמָלֵק֙ וְנִלְחַמְתָּ֣ בָ֔ם עַד־כַּלּוֹתָ֖ם אֹתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD sent thee on a journey, and said, Go and utterly destroy the sinners the Amalekites, and fight against them until they be consumed.
verses.rendering: The LORD sent you on a mission and said, 'Go and devote to destruction the sinners — the Amalekites. Fight against them until you have finished them off.'
translator_notes: The phrase vayyishlachakha YHWH baderekh ('the LORD sent you on a road/mission') uses the same verb as verse 1 (shalach, 'send') — Samuel recapitulates the commission. The designation hachatta'im ('the sinners') applies a moral category to the Amalekites — they are not merely enemies but offenders against God. The phrase ad-kallotam otam ('until you finish them off, until you consume them') from k-l-h ('to complete, finish, consume') demands total completion. Samuel is quoting back to Saul what God's command actually required — not the selective version Saul carried out, but the exhaustive one.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלָ֛מָּה לֹא־שָׁמַ֥עְתָּ בְּק֖וֹל יְהֹוָ֑ה וַתַּ֙עַט֙ אֶל־הַשָּׁלָ֔ל וַתַּ֥עַשׂ הָרַ֖ע בְּעֵינֵ֥י יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Wherefore then didst thou not obey the voice of the LORD, but didst fly upon the spoil, and didst evil in the sight of the LORD?
verses.rendering: So why did you not obey the voice of the LORD? Why did you pounce on the plunder and do what is evil in the LORD's eyes?"
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel's question strips away every layer of Saul's defense. The verb shamata ('you obeyed/listened') is second person singular — not 'why did the people disobey' but 'why did you.' The blame-shifting of verse 15 is rejected. The verb vatta'at ('you pounced, you swooped') from '-y-t depicts a bird of prey diving on its kill — the image is predatory, greedy, uncontrolled. Saul did not carefully set aside animals for sacrifice; he swooped on the plunder like a raptor. And the final verdict — vatta'as hara' be'einei YHWH ('you did the evil in the eyes of the LORD') — uses the definite article (hara', 'the evil'), suggesting not just a bad choice but the specific evil of covenant violation. In the Deuteronomistic framework, doing 'the evil in the LORD's eyes' is the standard formula for idolatry and apostasy.
translator_notes: The interrogative lammah ('why?') demands explanation. The phrase lo-shamata beqol YHWH ('you did not obey the voice of the LORD') is the chapter's central indictment, using the verb sh-m-' that Samuel introduced in verse 1. The verb vatta'at from '-y-t ('to swoop, pounce, rush greedily') appears also in 14:32, where the people 'pounced' on spoil after Saul's foolish oath — a recurring pattern of undisciplined greed. The phrase hara' be'einei YHWH ('the evil in the eyes of the LORD') is the Deuteronomistic judgment formula applied to Israel's worst kings.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֨אמֶר שָׁא֜וּל אֶל־שְׁמוּאֵ֗ל אֲשֶׁ֤ר שָׁמַ֙עְתִּי֙ בְּק֣וֹל יְהֹוָ֔ה וָאֵלֵ֕ךְ בַּדֶּ֖רֶךְ אֲשֶׁר־שְׁלָחַ֣נִי יְהֹוָ֑ה וָאָבִ֗יא אֶת־אֲגַג֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ עֲמָלֵ֔ק וְאֶת־עֲמָלֵ֖ק הֶחֱרַֽמְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: And Saul said unto Samuel, Yea, I have obeyed the voice of the LORD, and have gone the way which the LORD sent me, and have brought Agag the king of Amalek, and have utterly destroyed the Amalekites.
verses.rendering: Saul said to Samuel, "But I did obey the voice of the LORD! I went on the mission the LORD sent me on. I brought back Agag king of Amalek, and I devoted the Amalekites to destruction.
verses.expanded_rendering: Saul doubles down. Rather than repent, he insists: asher shamati beqol YHWH ('I did obey the voice of the LORD'). He repeats the exact verb Samuel just used to indict him, flipping it from accusation to defense. Then comes the inadvertent confession: va'avi et-Agag melekh Amaleq ('I brought Agag king of Amalek'). Saul mentions Agag's capture as though it were an achievement — he 'brought' the king as a trophy. He does not seem to understand that bringing Agag alive is itself the violation. In Saul's mental framework, capturing a king is a greater military accomplishment than killing one. He cannot see that God did not commission a trophy hunt but an execution. The claim ve'et-Amaleq hecheramti ('I devoted the Amalekites to destruction') is technically true of the common population — but partial truth in response to a total command is a lie.
translator_notes: The emphatic asher shamati ('indeed I obeyed') uses the relative pronoun asher as an emphatic particle — Saul is insisting, not conceding. The phrase va'elekh baderekh asher-shelachani YHWH ('I went on the road/mission the LORD sent me on') echoes verse 18 word for word, claiming complete compliance. The verb va'avi ('I brought') from b-w-' in the Hiphil ('to bring, to lead') treats Agag as acquired goods — something brought back from the campaign. The phrase ve'et-Amaleq hecheramti ('I devoted the Amalekites to destruction') is first person singular — Saul now claims personal credit for the cherem he actually carried out, even as he disclaims responsibility for the cherem he didn't.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּקַּ֨ח הָעָ֜ם מֵהַשָּׁלָ֗ל צֹ֤אן וּבָקָר֙ רֵאשִׁ֣ית הַחֵ֔רֶם לִזְבֹּ֖חַ לַיהֹוָ֣ה אֱלֹהֶ֑יךָ בַּגִּלְגָּֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: But the people took of the spoil, sheep and oxen, the chief of the things which should have been utterly destroyed, to sacrifice unto the LORD thy God in Gilgal.
verses.rendering: But the people took sheep and cattle from the plunder — the best of what was devoted to destruction — to sacrifice to the LORD your God at Gilgal."
verses.expanded_rendering: Saul again deflects blame to 'the people' and again frames the violation as sacrificial worship. But this time the language betrays him further: reshit hackerem ('the best/first portion of the devoted things'). The word reshit ('first, best, choicest') is the same word used for the first-fruits offering — Saul is describing cherem goods in sacrificial vocabulary, as though devoted-to-destruction items could be redirected to the altar. This is a categorical confusion: cherem goods belong to God through destruction, not through sacrifice. You cannot steal from God's destruction pile, put it on God's altar, and call it worship. And once again: Elohekha — 'your God,' not 'my God.' Saul continues to speak of God as Samuel's deity.
translator_notes: The phrase reshit hacherem ('the first/best of the devoted things') is deeply problematic theologically. Reshit normally describes premium offerings (reshit bikkurim, 'first of the first-fruits,' Exodus 23:19), but hacherem ('the devoted things') are not eligible for sacrifice — they are already claimed by God for destruction. Saul treats cherem as interchangeable with sacrificial offerings, which is a fundamental misunderstanding of the categories. The repeated Elohekha ('your God') again distances Saul from covenant relationship with God. The location baGilgal ('at Gilgal') is where Saul's kingship was renewed (11:15) — the site of his coronation feast will now become the site of his condemnation.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵ֗ל הַחֵ֤פֶץ לַיהֹוָה֙ בְּעֹל֣וֹת וּזְבָחִ֔ים כִּשְׁמֹ֖עַ בְּק֣וֹל יְהֹוָ֑ה הִנֵּ֤ה שְׁמֹ֙עַ֙ מִזֶּ֣בַח ט֔וֹב לְהַקְשִׁ֖יב מֵחֵ֥לֶב אֵילִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel said, Hath the LORD as great delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices, as in obeying the voice of the LORD? Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice, and to hearken than the fat of rams.
verses.rendering: Samuel said, "Does the LORD delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as much as in obeying the voice of the LORD? To obey is better than sacrifice; to listen is better than the fat of rams.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the theological summit of 1 Samuel and one of the most consequential statements in the Hebrew Bible. Samuel's rhetorical question (hachefets laYHWH be'olot uzvachim kishmoa' beqol YHWH — 'does the LORD delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as much as in obeying the LORD's voice?') forces a comparison between ritual worship and covenant obedience. The answer is emphatic: shemoa' mizzevach tov — 'obeying is better than sacrifice.' The infinitive absolute shemoa' ('to obey, to hear') stands as the supreme covenant virtue. Samuel does not abolish sacrifice — the entire Levitical system remains valid — but he establishes an unbreakable hierarchy: obedience is the precondition that gives sacrifice meaning. Without obedience, sacrifice is not merely inadequate; it becomes the very thing God rejects. The parallel line — lehaqshiv mechelev eilim ('to listen is better than the fat of rams') — uses a different hearing verb (q-sh-v, 'to attend, to pay close attention') paired with chelev ('fat'), the portion of the sacrifice that belonged exclusively to God (Leviticus 3:16). Even the most sacred part of the offering — God's own portion — is subordinate to attentive obedience.
translator_notes: The noun chefets ('delight, desire, pleasure') asks what God actually wants — not what humans assume God wants. The pair olot uzvachim ('burnt offerings and sacrifices') covers the two major categories of offering. The infinitive absolute shemoa' ('to obey/hear') functions as a verbal noun — obedience as a concept, a way of life. The adjective tov ('good, better') establishes the comparison: obedience is tov ('better') than sacrifice. The verb lehaqshiv from q-sh-v ('to attend, to listen attentively, to heed') adds nuance — this is not just hearing but careful, focused attention. The chelev eilim ('fat of rams') refers to the suet surrounding the kidneys and liver, the choicest sacrificial portion reserved for God alone (Leviticus 3:3-4, 3:16). Samuel's point: even the fat — the part that is entirely God's — is less important to God than a listening heart.

key_terms.semantic_range: to hear, to listen, to obey, to heed, to understand
key_terms.note: The infinitive absolute of sh-m-' here functions as the supreme theological principle of the chapter: obedience (hearing that produces action) is the foundation of all acceptable worship. This statement becomes the basis for the entire prophetic critique of ritual without ethics (Hosea 6:6, Amos 5:21-24, Isaiah 1:11-17, Micah 6:6-8). The Hebrew language does not separate hearing from obeying — genuine shema' always results in faithful action.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י חַטַּאת־קֶ֙סֶם֙ מֶ֔רִי וְאָ֥וֶן וּתְרָפִ֖ים הַפְצַ֑ר כִּ֗י מָאַ֙סְתָּ֙ אֶת־דְּבַ֣ר יְהֹוָ֔ה וַיִּמְאָסְךָ֖ מִמֶּֽלֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: For rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft, and stubbornness is as iniquity and idolatry. Because thou hast rejected the word of the LORD, he hath also rejected thee from being king.
verses.rendering: For rebellion is the sin of divination, and defiance is wickedness and idolatry. Because you rejected the word of the LORD, the LORD has rejected you as king."
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel now equates Saul's disobedience with the pagan practices Israel was commanded to eradicate. Meri ('rebellion') is compared to qesem ('divination') — the attempt to manipulate divine power for human purposes. Haftsar ('defiance, pushiness, insistence') is compared to aven uterafim ('wickedness and household idols'). The logic is devastating: Saul thought he was worshiping God by offering sacrifices; Samuel says he was practicing divination and idolatry. The difference between worship and witchcraft is not the external form but the internal posture — obedience or self-will. Then comes the devastating wordplay on ma'as ('reject'): ma'asta et-devar YHWH ('you rejected the word of the LORD') produces vayyima'askha mimmelekh ('he has rejected you as king'). The punishment mirrors the crime using the identical verb root. Saul rejected; therefore Saul is rejected. The kingdom is being torn from him not for political failure but for theological rebellion.
translator_notes: The noun meri ('rebellion, defiance') from m-r-h characterizes willful disobedience against known commands. The noun qesem ('divination') refers to pagan techniques for discerning or manipulating the divine will — strictly forbidden in Deuteronomy 18:10. Samuel's equation is not metaphorical but categorical: rebellion against God's word and divination against God's sovereignty are the same sin — both attempt to impose human will on the divine realm. The noun haftsar ('defiance, insistence, pushiness') from p-ts-r describes aggressive stubbornness. Terafim ('household gods, idol figurines') are condemned throughout the Hebrew Bible (Genesis 31:19, Judges 17:5, 2 Kings 23:24). The verb ma'as ('reject, refuse, despise') creates the wordplay: ma'asta (you rejected) → vayyima'askha (he rejected you). The preposition mimmelekh ('from being king') specifies what is lost — not Saul's life, but his dynasty and kingship.

key_terms.note: Ma'as is the verb of divine rejection in the Hebrew Bible, carrying connotations of repudiation after evaluation — not hasty dismissal but considered judgment. The wordplay here (you rejected → he rejected you) establishes the principle that human rejection of God's word produces divine rejection of human authority. This same verb will appear in 16:1 when God tells Samuel to stop grieving over Saul because 'I have rejected him' (me'astiv), and in verse 26 of this chapter as the final declaration.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֨אמֶר שָׁא֤וּל אֶל־שְׁמוּאֵל֙ חָטָ֔אתִי כִּֽי־עָבַ֥רְתִּי אֶת־פִּֽי־יְהֹוָ֖ה וְאֶת־דְּבָרֶ֑יךָ כִּ֤י יָרֵ֙אתִי֙ אֶת־הָעָ֔ם וָאֶשְׁמַ֖ע בְּקוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Saul said unto Samuel, I have sinned: for I have transgressed the commandment of the LORD, and thy words, because I feared the people, and obeyed their voice.
verses.rendering: Saul said to Samuel, "I have sinned. I violated the command of the LORD and your instructions, because I was afraid of the people and I listened to their voice.
verses.expanded_rendering: At last Saul confesses — chatati ('I have sinned'). But even his confession reveals the depth of his problem. He says he feared the people (yareti et-ha'am) and listened to their voice (va'eshma' beqolam). The verb eshma' ('I listened/obeyed') is once again the pivotal shama': Saul obeyed the people's voice instead of God's voice. The king who was supposed to lead Israel under God's authority instead followed Israel's desires against God's authority. He feared the wrong party. This is the fundamental inversion of kingship: a king who fears his subjects more than his God is no longer functioning as God's representative but as the people's servant — the very danger Samuel warned about in chapter 8. The confession is real, but it comes too late and may be motivated more by fear of consequences than by genuine repentance.
translator_notes: The confession chatati ('I have sinned') uses ch-t-' ('to miss the mark, to sin'). The verb avarti ('I violated, I crossed over') from '-v-r means to transgress a boundary — he crossed a line God drew. The phrase pi-YHWH ('the mouth/command of the LORD') personifies God's command as speech from God's mouth. The phrase ve'et-devarekha ('and your words') acknowledges that Samuel's instructions carried divine authority. The verb yareti ('I feared') from y-r-' admits the wrong kind of fear — Saul feared the people rather than God. The phrase va'eshma' beqolam ('I obeyed their voice') uses the same shama' + qol construction that should have been directed at God (v1: shema' leqol divrei YHWH). Saul obeyed the wrong voice.

verses.text_hebrew: וְעַתָּ֕ה שָׂ֥א נָ֖א אֶת־חַטָּאתִ֑י וְשׁ֣וּב עִמִּ֔י וְאֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶ֖ה לַיהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Now therefore, I pray thee, pardon my sin, and turn again with me, that I may worship the LORD.
verses.rendering: Now please forgive my sin and come back with me so I can worship the LORD."
translator_notes: The imperative sa na' ('please carry, please bear') asks Samuel to lift the sin from Saul — literally to 'carry' (nasa') his transgression. The plea veshuv immi ('return with me') reveals Saul's deeper concern: he wants Samuel's public presence as prophetic endorsement. The phrase ve'eshtachaveh laYHWH ('so I may worship the LORD') uses the Hishtaphel of sh-ch-h ('to bow down, prostrate oneself'). Saul's request is ambiguous — does he want genuine worship, or does he want the appearance of prophetic approval? The fact that his concern shifts immediately to public image (v30) suggests the latter.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵל֙ אֶל־שָׁא֔וּל לֹ֥א אָשׁ֖וּב עִמָּ֑ךְ כִּ֤י מָאַ֙סְתָּ֙ אֶת־דְּבַ֣ר יְהֹוָ֔ה וַיִּמְאָסְךָ֣ יְהֹוָ֔ה מִהְי֥וֹת מֶ֖לֶךְ עַל־יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel said unto Saul, I will not return with thee: for thou hast rejected the word of the LORD, and the LORD hath rejected thee from being king over Israel.
verses.rendering: Samuel said to Saul, "I will not go back with you, because you rejected the word of the LORD, and the LORD has rejected you from being king over Israel."
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel refuses. The verdict is final. He repeats the ma'as wordplay from verse 23, now in its fullest form: ma'asta et-devar YHWH vayyima'askha YHWH miheyot melekh al-Yisra'el ('you rejected the word of the LORD, and the LORD has rejected you from being king over Israel'). The parallel is exact — Saul's rejection of God's word and God's rejection of Saul's kingship are presented as a single cause-and-effect chain expressed in the same verb. The refusal to accompany Saul (lo ashuv immakh, 'I will not return with you') is not personal pettiness — it is prophetic dissociation. If Samuel walks alongside Saul in public worship, it communicates divine approval. Samuel cannot endorse what God has condemned.
translator_notes: The declaration lo ashuv immakh ('I will not return with you') uses the same verb shuv ('return') from Saul's plea in verse 25. The ma'as wordplay is repeated with fuller specification: miheyot melekh al-Yisra'el ('from being king over Israel') rather than just mimmelekh (v23). The rejection is now explicitly over all Israel — Saul's authority over God's people is revoked. The repetition of the full formula (you rejected → the LORD rejected) emphasizes the irreversibility of the divine decision.

key_terms.note: The second occurrence of the ma'as wordplay in this chapter (see v23). Here the formula is expanded: 'the LORD has rejected you from being king over Israel' — not just from kingship in the abstract but from ruling God's specific covenant people. The rejection is relational: Saul can no longer mediate between God and Israel because he has severed his own obedience to God's word.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּסֹּ֥ב שְׁמוּאֵ֖ל לָלֶ֑כֶת וַיַּחֲזֵ֥ק בִּכְנַף־מְעִיל֖וֹ וַיִּקָּרַֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: And as Samuel turned about to go away, he laid hold upon the skirt of his mantle, and it rent.
verses.rendering: As Samuel turned to leave, Saul grabbed the edge of his robe and it tore.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the most physically charged moments in the narrative. Samuel turns to walk away — the prophetic dissociation made visible. Saul, desperate, seizes the kenaf me'ilo ('the edge/corner of his robe'). The fabric tears. The Hebrew is ambiguous about whose robe tears — the context suggests Samuel's robe tearing in Saul's grip, though some read it as Saul's own robe. Either way, the torn fabric becomes an instant prophetic sign that Samuel will interpret in the next verse. The tearing of a garment becomes a recurring symbol of torn kingship in Israel's history: Ahijah will tear a garment before Jeroboam (1 Kings 11:30-31). Here the sign is unplanned — Saul's desperate grasping produces the very symbol of what he is losing. He reaches for the prophet and tears the kingdom.
translator_notes: The verb vayyissov ('he turned') from s-v-v indicates Samuel physically rotating to walk away. The verb vayyachazek ('he grasped, he seized') from ch-z-q indicates forceful gripping — Saul is not gently touching but clutching. The kenaf me'ilo ('the edge/corner of his robe') refers to the hem or decorative border of the outer garment. The me'il is a significant garment — it is the robe Samuel's mother made for him yearly (2:19). The verb vayyiqqara' ('it tore') from q-r-' provides the word Samuel will use in his interpretation. The ambiguity of the subject (whose robe?) has generated centuries of commentary; the narrative logic favors Samuel's robe tearing in Saul's hand.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֵלָיו֙ שְׁמוּאֵ֔ל קָרַ֨ע יְהֹוָ֜ה אֶֽת־מַמְלְכ֧וּת יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל מֵעָלֶ֖יךָ הַיּ֑וֹם וּנְתָנָ֕הּ לְרֵעֲךָ֖ הַטּ֥וֹב מִמֶּֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel said unto him, The LORD hath rent the kingdom of Israel from thee this day, and hath given it to a neighbour of thine, that is better than thou.
verses.rendering: Samuel said to him, "The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and has given it to a neighbor of yours who is better than you.
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel seizes the torn fabric as a prophetic sign and interprets it: qara' YHWH et-mamlekut Yisra'el me'alekha hayyom ('the LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from upon you today'). The verb qara' ('tear, rip') moves from physical accident to divine metaphor — what just happened to the robe is what God is doing to the monarchy. The kingdom is being ripped away from Saul and given to re'akha hattov mimmekka ('your neighbor who is better than you'). This unnamed 'neighbor' is David, though he will not be identified until chapter 16. The word re'a ('neighbor, companion, friend') is deliberately vague — David could be anyone in Israel. The comparative hattov mimmekka ('the better one than you') does not mean morally perfect but more obedient, more responsive to God's voice. Saul's replacement criterion is not charisma or military skill but faithfulness.
translator_notes: The verb qara' ('to tear, to rip') verbally links the torn garment to the torn kingdom — the physical act becomes a sign-act (ot). The phrase mamlekut Yisra'el ('kingdom of Israel') is the political entity itself, not just the throne. The preposition me'alekha ('from upon you') images the kingdom as a garment worn by the king — God is stripping it off. The time marker hayyom ('today') makes the verdict present and immediate. The phrase lere'akha hattov mimmekka ('to your neighbor, the better one than you') introduces the unnamed successor. The word re'a can mean 'companion, friend, fellow, neighbor' — it is deliberately unspecific, prolonging the mystery of identity.

verses.text_hebrew: וְגַם֙ נֵ֣צַח יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל לֹ֥א יְשַׁקֵּ֖ר וְלֹ֣א יִנָּחֵ֑ם כִּ֣י לֹ֥א אָדָ֛ם ה֖וּא לְהִנָּחֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And also the Strength of Israel will not lie nor repent: for he is not a man, that he should repent.
verses.rendering: Moreover, the Enduring One of Israel does not lie and does not relent, for he is not a human being, that he should relent."
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the most theologically dense verses in the Hebrew Bible. Samuel introduces a divine title found nowhere else in Scripture: Netsach Yisra'el — variously rendered 'the Enduring One of Israel,' 'the Glory of Israel,' 'the Eternal One of Israel.' The word netsach can mean 'permanence, perpetuity, glory, endurance, victory.' Samuel is asserting something about God's fundamental nature: the Netsach Yisra'el does not lie (lo yeshaqer) and does not relent (lo yinnachem). But the verb yinnachem is from nacham — the same root used in verse 11, where God said nichamti ('I regret') that he made Saul king. The same verb is affirmed of God in verse 11 and denied of God here in verse 29. The text does not resolve this tension. Samuel appears to be saying that while God may express grief and relational pain (nacham in v11), God's settled decisions are not reversible by human pleading — God does not relent the way humans do, changing course under pressure. The distinction is between divine grief (which is real) and divine fickleness (which is not). God's rejection of Saul stands because it proceeds from God's enduring nature, not from a momentary reaction.
translator_notes: The title Netsach Yisra'el ('the Enduring One / Glory / Permanence of Israel') is a hapax as a divine title — it appears only here. The root n-ts-ch relates to perpetuity, victory, and endurance. The verb lo yeshaqer ('does not lie') from sh-q-r affirms divine truthfulness — God's word is reliable. The verb lo yinnachem ('does not relent') from n-ch-m is the Niphal ('he does not cause himself to relent'), directly contradicting the Niphal nichamti of verse 11 ('I regret'). The phrase ki lo adam hu lehinnachem ('for he is not a human being, that he should relent') grounds the assertion in ontology — divine and human responses are categorically different. The theological puzzle of nacham affirmed (v11) and denied (v29) of God within 18 verses has generated vast rabbinic, patristic, and modern commentary.

key_terms.semantic_range: repent, relent, regret, be grieved, change one's mind, be comforted
key_terms.note: The theological tension surrounding nacham in this chapter is one of the most discussed in biblical theology. Verse 11: God nacham (regrets) making Saul king. Verse 29: God does not nacham (relent). Verse 35: God nacham (regretted) making Saul king. The text refuses to harmonize these statements, presenting divine constancy and divine grief as simultaneously true. God is not indifferent to human failure (he genuinely grieves) but he is not fickle (his settled decisions do not reverse under pressure). Both truths are held without resolution.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר חָטָ֔אתִי עַתָּ֗ה כַּבְּדֵ֤נִי נָא֙ נֶ֤גֶד זִקְנֵֽי־עַמִּי֙ וְנֶ֣גֶד יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל וְשׁ֣וּב עִמִּ֔י וְהִשְׁתַּחֲוֵ֖יתִי לַיהֹוָ֥ה אֱלֹהֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Then he said, I have sinned: yet honour me now, I pray thee, before the elders of my people, and before Israel, and turn again with me, that I may worship the LORD thy God.
verses.rendering: Saul said, "I have sinned. But now, please honor me before the elders of my people and before Israel. Come back with me so I may bow down to the LORD your God."
verses.expanded_rendering: Saul repeats his confession — chatati ('I have sinned') — but immediately pivots to what he actually cares about: kabbedeni na' neged ziqnei-ammi veneged Yisra'el ('please honor me before the elders of my people and before Israel'). The verb kabbed ('honor, give weight to') from k-v-d reveals Saul's real concern. He is not devastated by his rupture with God; he is terrified of public humiliation. He wants Samuel to stand beside him before the elders so that the rejection does not become common knowledge — yet. This is a request to preserve appearances, to maintain the facade of prophetic endorsement even after the endorsement has been withdrawn. And again: laYHWH Elohekha — 'to the LORD your God.' Even in his plea, Saul cannot bring himself to claim God as his own.
translator_notes: The repeated chatati ('I have sinned') is verbally identical to verse 24, but the context shifts its meaning — this time it is followed not by repentance but by a request for political face-saving. The imperative kabbedeni ('honor me') from k-v-d ('to be heavy, to honor') asks Samuel to give him public weight/dignity. The phrase neged ziqnei ammi ('before the elders of my people') and veneged Yisra'el ('before Israel') specifies two audiences: the political leadership and the general populace. Saul wants to avoid both audiences learning of his rejection. The persistent Elohekha ('your God') rather than Elohai ('my God') is now devastating — after being told God has rejected him, Saul still speaks of God as belonging to Samuel's sphere, not his own.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּ֥שׇׁב שְׁמוּאֵ֖ל אַחֲרֵ֣י שָׁא֑וּל וַיִּשְׁתַּ֥חוּ שָׁא֖וּל לַיהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: So Samuel turned again after Saul; and Saul worshipped the LORD.
verses.rendering: So Samuel went back with Saul, and Saul bowed down to the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel relents — he accompanies Saul and allows him this final public act of worship. The text does not explain Samuel's change of mind. Perhaps he recognized that a complete public rupture would plunge Israel into chaos before God's alternative was ready. Perhaps compassion for the man he had anointed overcame prophetic severity. Perhaps God directed him to do so. The narrator does not comment. What is clear is that this is a concession, not a reversal — Samuel accompanies Saul but the rejection stands. Saul bows down (vayyishtachu) to the LORD, but the LORD has already spoken his verdict. This worship is accepted in form but changes nothing in substance.
translator_notes: The verb vayyashav ('he turned back, he returned') from sh-w-v reverses Samuel's earlier refusal (lo ashuv immakh, v26). The phrase acharei Sha'ul ('after Saul') positions Samuel as following Saul — a reversal of the proper prophetic relationship where the king follows God's prophet. The verb vayyishtachu ('he bowed down, he worshiped') from sh-ch-h is the Hishtaphel indicating prostration. Saul worships — but worship after disobedience, as the chapter has established, is not what God desires.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵ֗ל הַגִּ֤ישׁוּ אֵלַי֙ אֶת־אֲגַג֙ מֶ֣לֶךְ עֲמָלֵ֔ק וַיֵּ֣לֶךְ אֵלָ֔יו אֲגָ֖ג מַעֲדַנֹּ֑ת וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֲגָ֔ג אָכֵ֖ן סָ֥ר מַר־הַמָּֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: Then said Samuel, Bring ye hither to me Agag the king of the Amalekites. And Agag came unto him delicately. And Agag said, Surely the bitterness of death is past.
verses.rendering: Then Samuel said, "Bring Agag king of Amalek to me." Agag came to him in chains. And Agag said, "Surely the bitterness of death has turned away."
verses.expanded_rendering: The scene shifts abruptly to Agag. Samuel summons the Amalekite king — the man Saul should have killed. The word ma'adannot is notoriously difficult: it may mean 'in fetters/chains' (from a root meaning 'to bind'), 'cheerfully/pleasantly' (from a root meaning 'delicacy, pleasure'), or 'trembling/faltering.' The traditional reading 'delicately' (KJV) suggests Agag approached with false confidence; the alternative 'in chains' fits the context of a prisoner brought before a judge. Agag's statement — akhen sar mar-hammavet ('surely the bitterness of death has turned away') — seems to express relief, as though he believes the danger has passed. If he has been kept alive this long, perhaps he thinks he will remain alive. He is catastrophically wrong. Within moments Samuel will kill him.
translator_notes: The imperative haggishu ('bring near') from n-g-sh commands Agag's presentation — this is judicial language, bringing the accused before the judge. The word ma'adannot is a crux interpretum. It may derive from '-d-n ('to be delicate, to be pampered') yielding 'pleasantly, cheerfully'; from m-'-d-n related to 'bonds, fetters'; or be an adverbial form meaning 'haltingly, falteringly.' The LXX reads tremon ('trembling'). Context favors either 'in fetters' (a prisoner presentation) or 'cheerfully' (false confidence). Agag's statement akhen sar mar-hammavet uses akhen ('surely, truly') as an emphatic particle, sar ('has turned aside, departed') from s-w-r, and mar-hammavet ('the bitterness of death'). The phrase may also be read as a question: 'Has the bitterness of death truly passed?' — expressing hope rather than certainty.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר שְׁמוּאֵ֔ל כַּאֲשֶׁ֨ר שִׁכְּלָ֤ה נָשִׁים֙ חַרְבֶּ֔ךָ כֵּן־תִּשְׁכַּ֥ל מִנָּשִׁ֖ים אִמֶּ֑ךָ וַיְשַׁסֵּ֨ף שְׁמוּאֵ֧ל אֶת־אֲגָ֛ג לִפְנֵ֥י יְהֹוָ֖ה בַּגִּלְגָּֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel said, As thy sword hath made women childless, so shall thy mother be childless among women. And Samuel hewed Agag in pieces before the LORD in Gilgal.
verses.rendering: Samuel said, "Just as your sword made women childless, so your mother will be childless among women." And Samuel hacked Agag to pieces before the LORD at Gilgal.
verses.expanded_rendering: Samuel pronounces sentence in the form of a talionic declaration — exact retribution. The structure is ka'asher ... ken ('just as ... so'): as Agag's sword (charvekha) bereaved women of their children, so Agag's own mother will be bereaved of her son. The punishment fits the crime in precise verbal symmetry. Then Samuel personally executes the sentence: vayeshassef Shemu'el et-Agag ('Samuel hacked Agag to pieces'). The verb sh-s-f is rare and violent — it means to cleave, to chop, to hack apart. This is not a clean execution; it is butchery. The aged prophet does what the warrior-king would not. The phrase lifnei YHWH baGilgal ('before the LORD at Gilgal') frames the killing as a sacral act — this is the completion of cherem, the devoted destruction that Saul left unfinished, performed in God's presence at the site of Saul's kingship. Samuel functions simultaneously as priest, prophet, judge, and executioner.
translator_notes: The verb shikkelah ('made childless, bereaved') from sh-k-l is a devastating word in Hebrew — it describes the specific grief of a parent who has lost children. The structure ka'asher ... ken ('as ... so') establishes lex talionis (proportional justice). The phrase tishkal minnashim immekha ('your mother will be childless among women') makes the punishment personal and genealogical — Agag's line ends here. The verb vayeshassef from sh-s-f ('to hew, to hack, to cleave') appears only here and in uncertain form — it describes violent dismemberment. The phrase lifnei YHWH ('before the LORD') marks this as a sacral execution, the completion of the cherem that Saul failed to carry out. Gilgal, the site of Israel's first Passover in the land (Joshua 5:10) and Saul's kingship renewal (11:15), now hosts the execution that seals Saul's rejection.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֵּ֥לֶךְ שְׁמוּאֵ֖ל הָרָמָ֑תָה וְשָׁא֛וּל עָלָ֥ה אֶל־בֵּית֖וֹ גִּבְעַ֥ת שָׁאֽוּל׃
verses.text_kjv: Then Samuel went to Ramah; and Saul went up to his house to Gibeah of Saul.
verses.rendering: Samuel went to Ramah, and Saul went up to his home at Gibeah of Saul.
verses.expanded_rendering: The two men part. The narrator records their destinations with stark simplicity: Samuel to Ramah (his hometown and prophetic base), Saul to Gibeah (his royal seat). They walk in opposite directions. The geographic separation mirrors the relational rupture — prophet and king, once bound together by anointing oil and divine commission, now divided by disobedience. Each goes to his own place. The journey home is the loneliest moment in both men's stories: Samuel returns to grieve over the king he anointed; Saul returns to a throne God has already stripped of its divine mandate. Neither man will be the same.
translator_notes: Ramah (haRamatah, with directional heh) is Samuel's home base (7:17, 8:4). Gibeah of Saul (Giv'at Sha'ul) is identified as Saul's beit ('house/home') — still his hometown, not a royal capital. The verb alah ('went up') for Saul indicates ascending elevation — Gibeah sits on a hill. The parallel construction (Samuel went to X, Saul went to Y) creates a visual of two figures walking apart, a narrative image of permanent separation.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלֹא־יָסַ֨ף שְׁמוּאֵ֜ל לִרְא֤וֹת אֶת־שָׁאוּל֙ עַד־י֣וֹם מוֹת֔וֹ כִּֽי־הִתְאַבֵּ֥ל שְׁמוּאֵ֖ל אֶל־שָׁא֑וּל וַיהֹוָ֣ה נִחָ֔ם כִּֽי־הִמְלִ֥יךְ אֶת־שָׁא֖וּל עַל־יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And Samuel came no more to see Saul until the day of his death: nevertheless Samuel mourned for Saul: and the LORD repented that he had made Saul king over Israel.
verses.rendering: Samuel never went to see Saul again until the day of his death, for Samuel mourned over Saul. And the LORD regretted that he had made Saul king over Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: The chapter's final verse is one of the most emotionally devastating in the Hebrew Bible. Three statements, each carrying immense weight. First: Samuel never saw Saul again — lo-yasaf Shemu'el lir'ot et-Sha'ul ad-yom moto. The phrase ad-yom moto ('until the day of his death') is ambiguous — whose death? Most likely Samuel's, meaning the separation was permanent and lifelong. Second: ki-hit'abel Shemu'el el-Sha'ul — 'for Samuel mourned over Saul.' The verb hit'abel is the reflexive form used for mourning the dead. Samuel grieves for Saul as though he has already died. The man who spent all night interceding for Saul (v11) now enters a permanent state of grief — not anger, not self-righteousness, but sorrow for a man who had everything and lost it. Third: vaYHWH nicham ki-himlikh et-Sha'ul al-Yisra'el — 'and the LORD regretted that he had made Saul king over Israel.' The verb nicham returns for the third and final time, closing the theological bracket opened in verse 11. Despite verse 29's assertion that God 'does not relent,' the narrator reports that God regretted. The tension remains unresolved. The chapter ends with two griefs — Samuel's and God's — both directed at the same man, both expressing the anguish of love that has been betrayed by the one it elevated.
translator_notes: The verb lo-yasaf ('he did not again, he never again') from y-s-f with the negative indicates permanent cessation. The phrase lir'ot et-Sha'ul ('to see Saul') means purposeful visit — Samuel did not seek Saul out. The phrase ad-yom moto ('until the day of his death') creates ambiguity: 'his' could refer to Samuel or Saul. Given that Samuel and Saul do meet again in 19:24 (at Naioth in Ramah, though that encounter is not initiated by Samuel) and in 28:11-19 (posthumously, at En-dor), the verse likely means Samuel never again sought Saul out in an official prophetic capacity. The verb hit'abel from '-v-l ('to mourn') in the Hitpael describes prolonged, active mourning — the kind displayed for the dead. The final clause vaYHWH nicham ('and the LORD regretted') uses the same Niphal of n-ch-m as verse 11 (nichamti), forming an inclusio around the chapter. The narrator gives the last word to divine grief — not divine anger, not divine punishment, but divine sorrow.

key_terms.semantic_range: repented, relented, regretted, was grieved, changed course
key_terms.note: The third and final occurrence of nacham applied to God in this chapter. Verse 11: 'I regret making Saul king.' Verse 29: 'God does not relent.' Verse 35: 'The LORD regretted making Saul king.' The chapter opens and closes with divine nacham, framing the entire narrative within the mystery of a God who is both unchanging in purpose and genuinely grieved by human failure. The text does not choose between these truths — it holds them together, leaving the reader inside the tension.

The Hitpael of '-v-l describes active, sustained mourning — typically mourning for the dead. Samuel mourns for Saul as though he were already dead: the Saul who could have been, the humble young man who became a disobedient king, is gone. This is not political disappointment but personal grief from a prophet who loved the king he anointed and now must live with the consequences of divine rejection.
